surface.name: K3s OSS Quick Start through a functional single-node cluster
surface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official K3s OSS Quick Start is primary because it is a public, no-account, one-command local Linux route. It is faster and less gated than Rancher Prime or SLES and explicitly states that the result is a fully functional single-node Kubernetes cluster.

alternatives_considered.name: SUSE Linux Enterprise Server
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer operating-system product with installation and administration journeys rather than the declared company-wide developer default.

alternatives_considered.name: K3s
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer cloud-native product and is also used as an implementation prerequisite inside the selected Rancher candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy Rancher on Amazon EKS
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Rancher deployment index presents EKS, Equinix Metal, manual Helm, and AWS SaaS as alternative routes without a universal provider choice.

entry_point.developer_intent: Choose a SUSE developer product and reach the first terminal state that its official quick-start documentation names.
assumed_prior_state: No SUSE product choice, Rancher installation, Kubernetes cluster, kubeconfig, Helm repository, cert-manager installation, domain, or Rancher administrator password is assumed.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Launch a fully functional single-node K3s cluster
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The K3s install script starts a single-node Kubernetes cluster and installs a configured `kubectl`.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: `sudo k3s kubectl get nodes` shows the local node in Ready state.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: A single-node server installation is a fully-functional Kubernetes cluster.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: A Ready node is the first observable proof that the K3s service, control plane, datastore, kubelet, and container runtime are running.

prerequisites.requirement: Provide a Linux virtual machine reachable from the workstation; the guide says not to install on localhost and requires saving the machine's IP address.
Select a supported Kubernetes version and have kubectl and Helm installed on the local workstation.

An authenticated SUSE Customer Center session is required to open the Prime-only documentation and retrieve the Rancher Prime Helm chart repository URL; the public guide does not enumerate account creation, access approval, or the hidden URL.
Have root or equivalent sudo access on the Linux virtual machine and permission to copy its K3s kubeconfig to the workstation.
Allow the workstation and browser to reach the Linux node on the Kubernetes API and web-service ports used by the guide.
Choose either the guide's IP-based sslip.io hostname or a real domain that points to the Linux node.

primary_path.action: Open the official K3s Quick Start.
details: The route requires no account or Prime entitlement.
primary_path.output: The one-command install route is visible.

primary_path.action: On a requirements-compatible Linux host, run `curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -`.
details: The script installs K3s as a service, adds kubectl and related utilities, and writes `/etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml`.
primary_path.input: Linux host with root privileges and network access
primary_path.output: The K3s service and single-node cluster start.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Check system requirements, privileges, service status, and installer output.

primary_path.action: Run `sudo k3s kubectl get nodes` and verify the local node is Ready.
primary_path.output: The cluster node table is displayed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Inspect the K3s service and logs if the node is not Ready.

branches.condition: A different SUSE product is intended
branches.path: Use that product family's own setup or quick-start documentation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the complete journey and terminal success rather than merely changing one step.

branches.condition: A managed or provider-specific Rancher route is preferred
branches.path: Choose Amazon EKS, Equinix Metal, or Rancher for AWS SaaS instead of the Helm CLI route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds provider-specific accounts, infrastructure, access, and commands.

friction_gates.description: SUSE exposes many peer product families without a company-wide default developer path.
Rancher itself offers multiple deployment quick starts with different provider and account requirements.
The required Rancher Prime chart repository URL is available only through Prime-only documentation that redirects to SUSE identity authentication.

sources.title: SUSE Documentation
evidence_supported: Absence of one company-wide starting route

sources.title: SUSE Rancher Prime Deployment Quick Start Guides
sections_used: SUSE Rancher Prime Deployment Quick Start Guides

sources.title: Helm CLI Quick Start
sections_used: Save Your Linux Node IP Address

excluded_after_success: Logging in, changing the bootstrap password, importing downstream clusters, deploying workloads, production certificates, backups, high availability, monitoring, and production hardening occur after the Rancher UI first appears.
